title: The lungs — how you breathe and exchange gases
examTip: Gas exchange is a major AQA topic. You need to name the structures (trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli), describe both inspiration and expiration with pressure changes, list the alveoli adaptations and explain each one, and describe how smoking damages the system.
introTips: Every cell in your body needs oxygen and produces carbon dioxide — the lungs are the interface that swaps one for the other, continuously, 15 times a minute.
AQA asks about the mechanism of breathing (pressure changes, muscle movements) and about why alveoli are so well adapted — you need both.
The smoking question comes up regularly, and you need to link specific chemicals (tar, carbon monoxide, nicotine) to specific effects.
intro: Right now, without thinking about it, you're breathing about 15 times a minute. Each breath pulls fresh oxygen into your blood and pushes out carbon dioxide — a gas exchange happening across hundreds of millions of tiny air sacs in your lungs. By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to explain exactly how that works, why the lungs are built the way they are, and what smoking does to all of it.

keyPoints.heading: The breathing system — the route air takes
keyPoints.content: Air enters through your nose or mouth and travels down the trachea (windpipe), which splits into two bronchi — one going to each lung. Each bronchus branches into smaller tubes called bronchioles, which end in tiny air sacs called alveoli (singular: alveolus). This branching structure massively increases the surface area available for gas exchange. The whole system is controlled by two sets of muscles: the intercostal muscles between the ribs, and the diaphragm below the lungs.
writeBullets: Route: trachea → bronchi (×2) → bronchioles → alveoli
Trachea: supported by rings of cartilage to keep it open
Bronchi: one to each lung
Bronchioles: smaller tubes branching from bronchi
Alveoli: tiny air sacs where gas exchange actually happens
Intercostal muscles (between ribs) + diaphragm (below lungs) = breathing muscles

keyTerms.def: The windpipe — the main airway from the throat to the bronchi, kept open by cartilage rings
The two main branches of the trachea, one leading to each lung
Smaller tubes branching from the bronchi, leading to the alveoli
Tiny air sacs at the end of the bronchioles where gas exchange between air and blood takes place
A dome-shaped sheet of muscle below the lungs; contracts and flattens during inhalation

keyPoints.memTip: Learn the route as a chain: Trachea → Bronchi → Bronchioles → Alveoli. A useful way to remember the order is that the words get longer as you go in (Bronchi → Bronchioles) then end with Alveoli. Say it aloud three times — it becomes automatic.

keyPoints.heading: Ventilation — how breathing works
keyPoints.content: Inspiration (breathing in): the diaphragm contracts (flattens), and the intercostal muscles contract, pulling the ribcage up and out. This increases the volume of the chest cavity, which decreases pressure inside it. Because pressure outside is now higher than inside, air rushes into the lungs. Expiration (breathing out): the diaphragm and intercostals relax, the ribcage moves down and in, volume decreases, pressure increases, and air is pushed out. Normal expiration is passive — it needs no muscular effort.
writeBullets: Inspiration: diaphragm contracts (flattens) + intercostals contract → ribcage up & out → volume increases → pressure drops → air rushes IN
Expiration: diaphragm relaxes + intercostals relax → ribcage down & in → volume decreases → pressure rises → air pushed OUT
Key principle: increased volume = decreased pressure; decreased volume = increased pressure
Normal expiration is passive (no muscle contraction needed)
Forced expiration (e.g. blowing hard) does use muscles

keyTerms.def: Breathing in — diaphragm and intercostal muscles contract, thorax volume increases, pressure falls, air enters
Breathing out — diaphragm and intercostal muscles relax, thorax volume decreases, pressure rises, air leaves
Muscles between the ribs that move the ribcage during breathing

keyPoints.memTip: The logic is always: volume up → pressure down → air in. Volume down → pressure up → air out. If you remember that, you can reconstruct all the muscle movements. A common exam trap: the diaphragm CONTRACTS (not relaxes) to breathe in — it flattens, pulling the floor of the chest down. Relax = expiration.

keyPoints.heading: Gas exchange at the alveoli
keyPoints.content: The alveoli are where the actual gas swap happens. Oxygen diffuses from the air in the alveolus (high O₂) into the blood in the capillaries (lower O₂). Carbon dioxide diffuses in the opposite direction — from the blood (high CO₂) into the alveolus (lower CO₂), and is then breathed out. This works by simple diffusion, so the alveoli need to maintain a steep concentration gradient. Their adaptations make this as efficient as possible: millions of alveoli give a huge surface area; walls one cell thick (squamous epithelium) give a very short diffusion distance; a rich capillary network constantly carries gases away, maintaining the gradient; and a moist lining allows gases to dissolve before diffusing.
writeBullets: O₂ diffuses: alveolus (high) → blood capillary (low)
CO₂ diffuses: blood capillary (high) → alveolus (low) → breathed out
Both directions happen simultaneously by diffusion
Alveoli adaptations: millions of alveoli (large SA), one-cell-thick walls (short diffusion distance), rich blood supply (maintains gradient), moist lining (gases dissolve)

keyTerms.def: The process by which oxygen moves from the lungs into the blood and carbon dioxide moves from the blood into the lungs
The difference in concentration of a substance across a surface — diffusion moves substances down this gradient
Flattened, single-layer cells forming the ultra-thin walls of the alveoli

keyPoints.memTip: Gas exchange is just diffusion — O₂ in, CO₂ out. Both move DOWN their own concentration gradients. The alveolus is designed to keep those gradients as steep as possible: thin walls (short distance), blood supply (constantly removing O₂ and delivering CO₂ to be expelled), huge SA (lots of diffusion happening at once). Link every adaptation to 'maintaining the concentration gradient' and you'll score full marks.

keyPoints.heading: Why the alveoli are so well adapted
keyPoints.content: Each alveolar adaptation directly supports efficient diffusion. Millions of alveoli → large surface area: more diffusion can happen simultaneously — the total SA of human alveoli is roughly 70 m², about the size of a tennis court. Walls one cell thick → short diffusion distance: gases only need to cross two thin cells (alveolus wall + capillary wall) to get from air to blood. Rich blood supply → steep concentration gradient: blood continuously removes O₂ from the capillary and delivers CO₂, so the gradient never flattens out. Moist lining → gases dissolve: O₂ and CO₂ must dissolve in water before they can diffuse across the membrane.
writeBullets: Large SA (millions of alveoli) → more diffusion surface → faster overall gas exchange
Thin walls (one cell thick) → short diffusion distance → gases cross membrane quickly
Rich capillary supply → maintains steep concentration gradient → diffusion continues rapidly
Moist lining → gases dissolve → enables diffusion across cell membranes

keyTerms.def: The total area of a surface available for exchange — larger SA means more diffusion at once
The distance a molecule must travel to cross a membrane — shorter distance means faster diffusion

keyPoints.memTip: For any adaptation question, use the formula: ADAPTATION → EFFECT → why it helps diffusion. 'Thin walls → short diffusion distance → gases cross quickly.' 'Rich blood supply → maintains concentration gradient → diffusion continues at a high rate.' Practise writing one sentence for each of the four adaptations — those are the marks.

keyPoints.heading: Smoking and the lungs
keyPoints.content: Tobacco smoke contains three key harmful substances. Tar coats the airways, irritates the lining, and is carcinogenic (causes lung cancer). It also damages cilia — the tiny hairs that sweep mucus and trapped particles out of the lungs. Without working cilia, mucus builds up and bacteria accumulate, causing chronic bronchitis. Over time, the alveoli walls break down (emphysema), drastically reducing surface area and making breathing very difficult. Carbon monoxide binds to haemoglobin in red blood cells more strongly than oxygen does, reducing the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity. Nicotine is addictive and raises heart rate and blood pressure, increasing the risk of cardiovascular disease.
writeBullets: Tar: carcinogenic (lung cancer), damages/destroys cilia, mucus builds up, bacteria trapped → bronchitis
Emphysema: alveoli walls break down → reduced surface area → less gas exchange → breathlessness
Carbon monoxide: binds haemoglobin instead of O₂ → reduced oxygen-carrying capacity of blood
Nicotine: addictive, raises heart rate and blood pressure → increased cardiovascular disease risk
Damaged cilia → mucus not cleared → 'smoker's cough' as body tries to shift mucus manually

keyTerms.def: A substance that causes cancer — tar in cigarette smoke is a carcinogen
Tiny hair-like structures lining the airways that sweep mucus and debris upward and out of the lungs
A lung disease caused by destruction of the alveoli walls, leading to reduced surface area and breathlessness
A toxic gas in cigarette smoke that binds to haemoglobin and reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen
The addictive chemical in tobacco; raises heart rate and blood pressure

keyPoints.memTip: Three chemicals, three effects. Tar → destroys cilia and causes cancer. Carbon monoxide → steals haemoglobin from O₂. Nicotine → addictive, heart rate up. For emphysema: the alveoli WALLS BREAK DOWN → LESS SURFACE AREA → LESS GAS EXCHANGE. Always say what happens structurally, then what the consequence is. That two-step answer is what AQA marks.

tables.title: Alveoli adaptations and their functions
rows: Very large total surface area (~70 m²)
More O₂ and CO₂ can diffuse at the same time
Gases cross from air to blood very quickly
Blood constantly flows past the alveoli
Maintains steep concentration gradient — O₂ removed, CO₂ delivered
Thin film of moisture on the inside surface
Gases dissolve before diffusing across the membrane

2.question: During inspiration (breathing in), what happens to the volume of the chest cavity and the air pressure inside it?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — as the diaphragm flattens and ribcage moves up and out, the chest gets bigger, pressure drops, and air rushes in.
2.wrongFeedback: When the diaphragm contracts (flattens) and the intercostals pull the ribcage up and out, the chest cavity INCREASES in volume. By Boyle's law, more space means lower pressure — so air rushes IN from higher pressure outside. Volume up → pressure down → air in.

3.question: Why is a rich blood supply to the alveoli important for efficient gas exchange?
options: It makes the alveoli walls thinner
It increases the surface area of the alveoli
It maintains a steep concentration gradient by continuously removing O₂ and delivering CO₂
It keeps the alveoli moist so gases can dissolve
3.correctFeedback: Exactly — the blood continuously carries O₂ away and brings CO₂ in, keeping the concentration difference steep so diffusion keeps happening rapidly.
3.wrongFeedback: The blood supply matters because it maintains the concentration gradient. If blood sat still, O₂ would build up in the capillary and diffusion would slow down. Constant blood flow keeps O₂ concentration low in the capillary and CO₂ concentration high, so diffusion continues rapidly in both directions.

commonMistakes: Saying the diaphragm RELAXES to breathe in — it's the opposite. The diaphragm CONTRACTS (flattens) during inspiration. It relaxes during expiration.
Confusing volume and pressure — when volume increases, pressure decreases. Always state both together: 'volume increases, pressure decreases, air moves in'.
Saying oxygen is 'pumped' into the blood — it diffuses. Gas exchange at the alveoli is always diffusion, moving down a concentration gradient. Never say 'pumped' for gas exchange.
Listing adaptations without explaining them — saying 'thin walls' alone is not enough. You must say 'thin walls → short diffusion distance → gases cross quickly'. Link adaptation to function.
Mixing up the effects of tar and carbon monoxide — tar damages cilia and causes cancer. Carbon monoxide reduces oxygen-carrying capacity by binding to haemoglobin. Don't swap them.
examTips: When AQA asks you to 'describe and explain ventilation', write separate paragraphs for inspiration and expiration. For each, state what the muscles do, what happens to volume, what happens to pressure, and which direction air moves — four points per paragraph.

The alveoli adaptation question is nearly always worth 4 marks. Aim for four adaptations, each with a function. The safest format is: '[Adaptation] — this means [structural effect], which [increases/decreases/maintains] [feature], so [diffusion outcome]'.

Smoking questions often ask you to 'explain how smoking causes breathlessness'. The route is: tar → cilia damaged → mucus builds up AND/OR emphysema → alveoli walls break down → reduced surface area → less O₂ enters blood → breathlessness. Write it as a chain of cause and effect.

revisionCardBullets: Route of air: trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli
Inspiration: diaphragm contracts (flattens) + intercostals contract → volume up → pressure down → air IN
Expiration: diaphragm relaxes + intercostals relax → volume down → pressure up → air OUT
Gas exchange: O₂ diffuses alveolus → blood; CO₂ diffuses blood → alveolus (both by diffusion)
Alveoli adaptation 1: millions of alveoli → large surface area → more diffusion at once
Alveoli adaptation 2: walls one cell thick → short diffusion distance → fast gas exchange
Alveoli adaptation 3: rich blood supply → maintains steep concentration gradient
Alveoli adaptation 4: moist lining → gases dissolve before diffusing
Tar: damages cilia, causes cancer, leads to mucus build-up and emphysema (destroyed alveoli → less SA)
Carbon monoxide: binds haemoglobin → reduces oxygen-carrying capacity of blood
